text: so hello everyone this is the part two of our video on mesometrial resection done laparoscopically and here we'll be describing the preservation of the nerves and the removal of the entire peritoneum these two are extremely important components which need to be done as a part of this entire procedure

As I have always stated, this entire procedure is going to be performed simply using the laparoscopic shearer and the Bissinger bipolar. Both of them will continue to act as devices for traction and counter traction simultaneously.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the hypogastric nerve, ensuring its preservation to maintain bladder, bowel, and sexual function post-surgery.
The surgeon has completely separated the peritoneum from the retroperitoneum, preparing to remove the peritoneum as part of the embryological component of the procedure.

the dissection needs to be very very gentle which is very important one need not be very aggressive in the dissection in this plane because it is a completely bloodless plane that is something which all of us need to remember also when we are taking

The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the peritoneum, taking care to include a portion of the para rectal fat to ensure a good margin. The dissection is shown as an unedited procedure, highlighting the absence of nerve supply in the para rectal fat, which does not affect bowel function.

The surgeon performs a gentle dissection, following anatomical planes to open them up progressively. This technique liberates the tissue margins, allowing the removal of the entire tissue content as a single specimen after mesometrial resection.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device and laparoscopic shearer to apply traction and perform rapid, bloodless dissection in the surgical field.

The surgeon applies cranial traction to the peritoneum, visibly opening and liberating it from surrounding structures. The peritoneum appears thickened, a common observation in Indian women due to high rates of pelvic inflammation.
So that is something which all the surgeons who plan to do these type of surgeries will have to get used to because that is exceedingly common in our Indian population.

the exact same steps which are being done for the right side need to be repeated on the left side as well and through the part and the course of these videos we will be only demonstrating the right side and the left side if it is similar will be omitted because it is a very long surgery for any member who wants to view the complete surgery it has been demonstrated on our facebook live page the whole surgery live was demonstrated on our facebook live page so members who want to go through that

The surgeon continues the dissection to strip off the entire peritoneum, exposing the bare rectum. The pararectal fossa is dissected, revealing the ureter up to the ureteric tunnel. The uterine artery is visible at its origin, prepared for coagulation.

The hypogastric nerve plexus is visible caudal to the ureter, serving as an important anatomical landmark. The uterine artery at its origin is used as a margin of the vascular parametrium and is being coagulated.
The surgeon uses the Bissinger bipolar device to apply short and gentle bursts of energy, effectively coagulating the uterine artery at its origin.

One can also notice in this plane the importance of traction. I have mentioned this again and again. I must also repeat here that all the hysterectomies and surgeries which are being performed at our institute are surgical proceedings which are done without the use of any uterine manipulator.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum medial to the ureter, completing the ureteric dissection up to the level of the ureteric tunnel. The peritoneum is carefully separated through dissection.

The surgeon achieves peritoneal separation and gently pushes the rectum away, ensuring the entire peritoneum covering is liberated and removed with the specimen. The rectum is visible, and the peritoneum is carefully lifted to prevent damage to the rectal serosa during dissection.
The surgeon lifts the peritoneum, mobilizing the rectum on one side, demonstrating careful dissection techniques to avoid damage to surrounding structures.

The surgeon preserves the pararectal fat while lifting the peritoneum flush to the rectum, revealing a large margin of liberated peritoneum from the pouch of Douglas, which will be removed with the specimen.
Approximately two centimeters of peritoneum is visibly uplifted, highlighting a critical technical aspect of the surgical procedure.

The same proceedings are going to be done on the opposite side as well. We will not demonstrate the entire proceedings for saving the time.
